text: The patient is in dorsal decubitus position with a cushion in his back.
Draping includes the right ground in case of extracorporeal life support.
A median sternotomy is performed.
Then, the sternal spreader is opened carefully because of a hardened mass in the upper portion of the incision.
Progressive dissection of the tumour from the pericardium which is opened.
Pre-pericardial fat is completely removed.
Opening of the right pleural cavity in order to visualize the right phrenic nerve which was found invaded.
Internal thoracic vessels are isolated and clip-legated literally to the tumour.
The pericardium is open on its right side to mobilize the mass.
The left pleural cavity is open, allowing us to identify the left phrenic nerve which is in direct contact with the tumor but does not seem to be invaded by it.
Once it was fully dissected, its normal motor function was demonstrated by direct stimulation.
The left pleural is open superiorly and the left brachiocephalic vein is encircled and sectioned by an endo-GIA vascular stapler.

The tumor mass is fully separated from the aortic arch by resecting the anterior pericardium with insufficient margin that allow us to completely mobilize the tumor to the right and to free it from the mediastinal or large vessels.
We dissected medial portion of brachiocephalic vein and thoroughly the venous stasis is assured.
In view of a clearly invaded superior vena cava, we decide to resect it.
Venous bridging was carried out before clamping the superior vena cava.
After clamping the left brachiocephalic vein, the stepper line is removed.
We use a 12 mm range cortex prosthesis.
The proximal anastomosis is carried out with a 6-0 Proline continuous suture.
The right atrium is free so that it can be clamped laterally.
The atrium is open and muscular tuberculi are sectioned to allow a free blood flow.
The graft is de-haired and progressively filled.
This is an anterior view of a fully functional bypass.
We dissect on the right upper part of the tumour mass in order to free the right brachiocephalic vein and the proximal part of the internal thoracic artery to section them.
Similarly, the zygous vein is ligated distally since its connection to the superior vena cava was also invaded.
The tumour is dislocated inferiorly allowing us to staple the superior vena cava along with cavo-atrial junction.
The right phrenic nerve was sectioned once we concluded it could not be safely separated from the tumour and its contralateral contralateral was fully functional.
The tumour is now only attached to the lung.
Aherences are freed far from the lesion and limited pulmonary ridge resection is performed while preserving the upper lobe veins.
Since the pulmonary vessels are not invaded, we can perform a lung sparing resection.
A complete margin-free en bloc resection is achieved.
A posterior view shows several anatomical references.
Finally, we perform a right paratracheal lymph node dissection and the pericardial margins are enlarged.
This is an anterior view of the surgical field with the preserved left phrenic nerve and other vascular mediastinal structures such as right pulmonary artery.
The anterior pericardium is reconstructed by a loosely placed vicryl mesh.
